sentences.text: Right.
So that happened.
In fact, that must have happened later that-- than I maybe gave the impression before.
I think it happened only in the late 1941, probably in autumn or thereabout of 1941.
And we had no place to move to to start.
And we lived-- for a while I think we lived with our family.
We lived with-- in [POLISH] Street, which I mentioned before, where most of the family of my mother lived.
And that was a flat, I think, on the ground floor, if remember correctly, not terribly big.

But it was all similar, to mind, it slightly goes together with the flat where the Szenfelds, the family of my mother, lived where my-- my grandparents, my maternal grandparents fled in [POLISH] Street, where they lived before the war.
It was another not terribly big flat.
But it was always full of people, because there were so many of members of the family.
There was my aunt Hania, who was teaching English in one room.
And there were three or four other members of the family, my uncle Yeziek and my uncle Sevek and [INAUDIBLE] and so on.
And they were in the front room discussing something and that was as well the place where they were eating, because there was a big table there.
And I was somehow very often there in those days.
I was even sleeping there, although there were so many people, I'm not quite sure anymore how I managed to sleep.
But I think one got used to everything, and I was even able to sleep with the light and with people being there all around me.
And there were as well my younger cousins.

There was-- there was Damka, who was my cousin who was then maybe 14 or 15, and was preparing for-- for her school exams, although I'm not quite sure what school, where she was doing it, if she was doing it all privately.
But I-- I enjoyed the atmosphere in [POLISH] because there were-- everybody was terribly full of-- of beans, so to say, and they were talking about many things and arguing and very lively.
And on the whole, they were always in good spirits, in spite of the situation being very unhappy.
But they always were in good spirits and-- and full of talking and full of interest.
I stayed as well in another flat in [POLISH], I think, in a-- where-- of another uncle, another brother of my mother.
Joachim was his-- where he lived with his wife and with my cousin Rutka.
And there were there as well I think other members of that family.
That was the family of-- of my uncle.
They were called Gesundheit and they lived there as well in the same flat.
I think there were three families living in the flat.
I think that was a large flat where only one family lived before the war and now there were probably three of them.

And that was, of course, a common thing in ghetto, that if you had family which lived before the war somewhere else, maybe outside Warsaw and part of Warsaw which was then not in the ghetto, you had willy-nilly to accept that the family moved in with you, because there was absolutely--it would be unthinkable to do anything else.
I didn't mention as well that when the ghetto was started, that was, of course, surrounded-- in the very beginning, it was surrounded by the barbed wire.
But later on, the wall was built.
I think already in 1940, in the winter of 1940 to 1941.
And that was a solid brick wall, which was probably well over two meters high with glass splinters embedded in the top layer so that it was very difficult to cross it.
And that was surrounding all of the ghetto.
And I was going in ghetto to school.
But the school-- I don't remember it so terribly well.
I know that I was going to-- in a street which was [POLISH] Street in the beginning, which was not very far from [POLISH].
And there I was going to the-- maybe in '40/'41 in winter '40/'41 and until the end of spring '41.
I was going I think to the second class, elementary school.
Well, yes, it was Jewish, because there was nobody else.

No, I don't think so, because if I would have learned any Yiddish or anything to do with the traditional things, I would have remembered it later, and I don't think I learned-- I certainly didn't learn anything remotely to do with religion or anything to do with Hebrew or anything to do with Yiddish.
I think it was a normal elementary school.
I must have read about the-- learned or read about the stories of the Bible, because I knew the stories of the Bible.
And I knew quite a bit of the Bible, of the early chapters and so on.
But that must have-- all of that must have been in Polish.
Yes, but I don't think I learned about the Bible in the school.
I think that was something that I learned-- I must have read, because I was reading a lot.
I could already read before the war started.
I learned to read already before the war, so they put me to second class.
But what they exactly were teaching I don't remember.
It was, of course, not an official school.
Those were classes where-- where things were being taught, but not an official school.
And I'm not quite sure even what the building was.
But schools there were, certainly.
And I went later on, in 1941, 1942 to another school.
And that was on the street called [POLISH] Liberty Street, which was right on the border of the ghetto.
One had to go a long way along the wall, in a-- along the wall of the ghetto.
That was right next to the wall.
And that was in a pretty empty part of the ghetto.
At least there were no-- I think there were relatively few people there.
And-- but I don't remember names of people there.
I remember slightly the classroom and [INAUDIBLE].
And of course, all the teaching was there as well in Polish.
And I think in fact that that school might have been something to do with this-- with some kind of a-- it might have been school for the converted Jews.
I'm not quite sure if it was, but I think that I remember something very, vaguely about it.
But I don't think there was any religion whatsoever learned one way or another.
Eight?
Eight, yes.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right.
And there was-- one other thing that I remember was that there was the orphanage of Korczak was not very far.
Yes.
Janusz Korczak was-- well, he was a well-known figure in the ghetto.
Everybody knew-- I knew very well who he was, because I read his books.
He wrote many books.
He wrote--
He was a writer.
He was a writer and he was a doctor and he was an educationalist.
And he wrote books in a whole series of books for children mainly.
And some of the-- and he had as well-- he was appearing in Polish radio, but that was before the war.
He had-- and I remember some of those-- the books were-- there was a book about Polish children and there was a book about-- I remember the names of those books.
There was book called [POLISH] Polish names.
And there was another book called [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] something like [POLISH] in other words, Jewish names.
[POLISH], it was Moses.
And [POLISH] was a small nickname of coming from Israel.
So-- and-- so that would-- because he had, of course, an orphanage for Jewish children.
Of course he was.
Of course he was.
Janusz Korczak was his-- was his-- pseudonym.
But his real name was Henryk Goldszmit.
Of course he was Jewish and the orphanage was Jewish and all- most of the staff or all of the staff in orphanage was Jewish.
And the orphanage existed for many years before the war.

And-- and Korczak had his own educational principles, which were basically principles that children have to be allowed to develop according to their natural principles, that children are naturally good, that one has to encourage in children their instincts of justice and fairness and so on.
And it was -- Korczak was famous.
During the war, I think they had great problems with supplies, with food, but they managed.
I think Korczak was so well-known that he had all kinds of people who were helping.
But they were as well doing all kinds of things.
Among other things they had this theater, where there was a marionette theater.
And there was a performance which I remember where I went to this marionette theater.
That must have been in 1941.
And Korczak was there himself, sitting next to the entrance.
And what the play was about I don't remember.
But I remember when we went out, Korczak kissed me on the forehead.
And that was for me already terribly important, because I knew who he was.
I read his books.
And I loved his books.
The books were really very nice.
So-- and I remember the beard and--
Clothes.
Right.
Yes.
Well, I don't remember my clothes in detail.
As for signs, I didn't carry anything.
There was a armband, which grown-ups had to carry.
I think I'm not quite sure what age you started to carry it, quite-- quite small, I think 12 maybe, or 12 or 14, something like that.
And that was already started before the ghetto was there.
That was already started in 1940.
That was already there, I think, when we came to Warsaw back from Bialystok and in spring.
I think it was already in '39.
And that was just a white armband with a blue Star of David.
Put on it to show that you are Jewish.
And that was something that, of course, people hated to wear, in particularly in the-- before the ghetto was formed, because for start, it was-- it was a sign of-- it was humiliating.
And the second thing was that you-- probably some people could be attacked by-- by Polish anti-Semites if that were-- but in ghetto, everybody wore it, everybody-- all the-- all the grown-ups.
But not me.
I think I was still-- when the ghetto came to an end, I still was much too young for-- for that.
Yes, we lived in a--
Sorry.
The grown-ups, some of the-- I must say that I'm not 100% aware to what extent they worked.
I think I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not so aware of that.
But they must have worked, because the-- the uncle who was a doctor, he certainly worked.
And the uncles who were having the-- who were working in the workshop, they worked.
They were there during the day, so they were working.
And-- but never went-- I was never in the place where they worked, so I was not aware of all that.
And the one that I remember that did work was-- if I remember seeing working who was a cousin, an older cousin, one of-- Bolek, who was a cousin of my mother.
And he was for a while conductor of one of those horse-coaches.

And when I was going to school in [POLISH] Street, in there, in probably it was already in early '42, that was-- I was going near to place where those-- the end station of that-- of that coach service.
And he would-- and he was sometimes there, and-- because he was a conductor.
And he would let me make-- go on the coach when it was making a loop round from the-- when it was turning round.
And that was something that I was very keen on.

Otherwise, I had-- then we moved in early '42, late '41 or early '42, something like that, my father did manage to find another flat in [POLISH], in the same street that I described before, but in another part of the street, which still was part of the ghetto in [POLISH].
That was another big house.
Somehow that was a nicer house than where we lived before.
And the flat was nicer.
It was maybe smaller, but it was quite nice.

And my parents somehow managed to decorate it and to get still furniture, still the same old furniture, still the same old sofa that we-- that was from before the war, that we still managed to drag-- my mother somehow managed to save it until there, a big sofa with dark green covers.
And that was still there, and some other bits of furniture, not many, but they were still there.
And there were about two rooms in this flat.
And somehow that flat was nice and I was keen on that.
And that was [POLISH].
And the whole area there, [POLISH] was on there.

We always managed to put ourselves-- I think that was my parents didn't want to go into the most crowded part, so they always tried for the, in inverted commas, 'nice' part, nice area, which was not very easy.
But they still-- it was a really relatively pleasant place, where there were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were-- the people who lived there, they were mostly professionals or colleagues of my father.
And in this-- in those courtyards, by now in some of those courtyards, people were terribly deprived of green and of fresh air, because there was none.
And there was this-- so people suddenly started digging up the courtyard and making little gardens in the courtyards with some vegetables.
I don't think there were many flowers, but there might have been some flowers.
And there certainly were vegetables and they were growing vegetables in those courtyards.
And I used to go into that garden and sit there and read, because I was absolutely crazy about reading, so I was always reading there, reading in this garden of the [POLISH].
And I really don't remember going more north of there into this very crowded area in the-- no, I was [POLISH] and so on, but not further north.
Anyway, there was in-- in this place in [POLISH], it was relatively nice.
And in-- all together, it was spring in '42, and things somehow seemed to be--
--a little bit better, not much better maybe, but a little bit better.
And we were still surviving.
And of course, the population of the ghetto must have gone a little down because so many people died.
So many people died of hunger.
In the ghetto, of course, of course.
There were-- people, masses of people who died every day, particularly during the winter.
And there were-- as I said, there were those carts of the [POLISH], which were collecting masses of bodies everyday.
And they were-- so some of the-- the poorest and some of the beggars died, and natural deaths, if one can call it natural death, in those winters.
And it was probably the conditions were a tiny bit-- for a while, they were a tiny bit better, better and more optimistic.
And in spring '42, we really thought that things were slightly normalizing, if not better than normalizing.
On the other hand, the Germans started the war with the Russians-- with Russia.
And we were very aware of that.
And I think most people were hoping that the Germans were== that Hitler was going to get eventually a bloody nose, that he was going to lose the war, but not everybody.
Some people thought the Germans were going to win the war.
And I remember that because there was this lawyer in the house, a friend of my father, Moshlonko, who was later a very famous lawyer in Poland after the war.
And he was generally highly respected because he was so clever and he knew everything.
And he was absolutely of the opinion that Germans were going to be-- the Germans were invincible and the Germans were going to win the war.

And other than that, we-- the Germans started war with the-- with Russia, and they moved so quickly east that-- for that at this time, I think still the Russians were surrendering and going back and going back and there was no sign yet that the Germans were going to suffer any defeat at all.
And we were, as well, not aware at this stage, although it was already '42, that there was something worse in-- being prepared or that worse things were happening, at least not quite consciously.
Maybe there were signs, but we didn't want to-- to listen to them then, although there was one thing, that my aunt, one of the-- Mania, who was a sister of my mother, older sister.
And she was a doctor.
She was a pediatrician.
I mentioned her before.
And she was a pediatrician.
And she was as well a quite experienced doctor.
And she went to the east as well in the beginning of the war.
But she didn't come back.
And she was with her husband, who she married briefly before the war, when I mentioned that there was a wedding, and I went to that wedding.
So she was not so young already.
She must have been in her late 30s at least.
And she was then a director of the hospital in Soviet-occupied part of Poland, in a place called [POLISH], and-- in a small town in East Poland.
And she must have taken the Soviet nationality, because she never went-- she was never deported.
She was just the director of this hospital in [POLISH].

And she was sending us packs, food packages to the ghetto, which were something which was terribly important, because we-- it was fantastic that there were still packages of-- it was honey and there was some tins.
And there was maybe butter I think she was sending.
And they were still arriving, those packets.
But after the war started with the Russians, briefly after, the packages--
--stopped coming and letters from Mania stopped coming and we lost contact with her.
And of course, we never knew anything what happened to her.

But of course, she was there, as most of the Jewish population in those areas, together with her husband, they were all shot together, the whole Jewish population of the town was-- we found about it after the war-- was gathered together and they were just shot, probably in early stages of the war, some time in summer 1941.
So-- but we didn't know.
We didn't know.
They just-- there was no contact.
There was nothing happening.
And-- but in '42, the things started happening which were strange.
There were suddenly-- they started in ghetto, the Germans started-- there were all kinds of signs which meant that things were not--
They're changing.
There were those films which I said-- I spoke about before.
They were filming.
They were coming and they were closing certain area.
And they were filming, as I said, Jews eating and Jews starving, or Jews dancing and having a good time or all kinds of strange things.
And then there were sudden-- and then they were catching people for work.
But they were catching-- they were catching people for work usually by the Jewish police already earlier in-- already in 1940, '41.
But it was-- it was happening now as well.
And they were-- and there were executions.
There were a few times that they were catching people.
They suddenly came to the ghetto, probably in spring '42.
And they suddenly caught a group of people.
And later it was I think group of about 100 people, something like, quite a lot.
And they were coming for them by name.
And they were usually well-known people like doctors, people whom they knew about by name.
And they were taking them and they shot all of them.
I think 100 hostages they shot on the one night.
That was already-- must have been in May or June 1942.
I think mostly men.
I'm not sure.
It could have been men and women, but I think mostly men.
And those were hostages.
And they might have shot them for some kind of reason that they later found-- I mean, there might have been something.
But there might-- I don't even think so.
I think that was just a pure act of terror that was unexplained and just to make people frightened.

And because, of course, at this time already must have been rumors, because horrible things were happening in other cities in-- in Poland before, like in east parts of Poland that were taken from the Russians, like in Lvov, where massacres started already a year practically before Warsaw.
And people were coming from Lvov.
But I was not aware.
Maybe they kept those things from me because they didn't want their children to know.
